dockerfile-00000.description:
No Dockerfile was found in the project. This suggests the application may not yet be containerized. 
Consider creating a Dockerfile to enable container-based replatforming to Azure services such as Azure Container Apps or AKS.
dockerfile-00000.title: No Dockerfile found

eclipse-00002.description:
Eclipse project detected. This project appears to use Eclipse-specific configurations, such as WTP or JEM natures, which can make build and deployment less portable in cloud environments.
Ii's recommended to migrate the project to a standard build tool like **Maven** for improved portability, reproducibility, and CI/CD automation.
eclipse-00002.title: Eclipse project found

hardcoded-urls-00001.description: This patterns verifies in source code and configuration files the presence of hardcoded URLs using the HTTP protocol (HTTP/HTTPS). This URL would need to be replaced by the new resource's URL during the Cloud migration, if any change occured.
hardcoded-urls-00001.title: Avoid using hardcoded URLs (HTTP protocol) in source code

local-storage-00001.description:
When migrating an application that relies on the file system to Azure, it's important to consider the limitations of local storage in a cloud environment.
 An application running inside a container could lose access to a file in local storage.

 The following recommendations depend on the function of the file in local storage:
 * Determine the **storage needs** for your application: Ephemeral storage or persistent storage. Ephemeral storage only persists until the cloud environment is restarted. Persistent storage is required for data that needs to persist across restarts.
 
 * If persistent storage is needed, consider **mounting Azure File Share** or **using Azure Blob Storage**. Mount Azure file share allows your application to access files via the mount location. Azure blob storage is a scalable object store for data objects, such as text or binary files.

 * **Logging**: Log to standard output and use Azure Monitor to collect and analyze the logs.

 * **Caching**: Use Azure Cache for Redis as a cache backing service.

 * **Configuration**: Store configuration in Azure App Configuration instead of using the file system. Also store configuration settings in environment variables so that they can be updated without code changes.
local-storage-00001.title: File system - Java IO

links.title: Create an Azure Files volume mount in Azure Container Apps
Use storage mounts in Azure Container Apps

local-storage-00002.description:
When migrating an application that relies on the file system to Azure, it's important to consider the limitations of local storage in a cloud environment.
 An application running inside a container could lose access to a file in local storage.

 The following recommendations depend on the function of the file in local storage:

 * Determine the storage needs for your application: Ephemeral storage or persistent storage. Ephemeral storage only persists until the cloud environment is restarted. Persistent storage is required for data that needs to persist across restarts.
 
 * If persistent storage is needed, consider using Azure File Share or Azure Blob Storage. Mount Azure file share allows your application to access files via the mount location. Azure blob storage is a scalable object store for data objects, such as text or binary files.

 * **Logging**: Log to standard output and use Azure Monitor to collect and analyze the logs.

 * **Caching**: Use Azure Cache for Redis as a cache backing service.

 * **Configuration**: Store configuration in Azure App Configuration instead of using the file system. Also store configuration settings in environment variables so that they can be updated without code changes.
local-storage-00002.title: File system - java.net.URL/URI

links.title: Create an Azure Files volume mount in Azure Container Apps
Use storage mounts in Azure Container Apps

localhost-00004.description: The app is trying to use localhost, please try to migrate the resource to cloud
localhost-00004.title: Localhost Usage

spring-boot-to-azure-spring-boot-version.description:
The application is using the End of OSS Support of Spring Boot Version. 
With the officially supported new versions from Spring, you can get the best experience. Here are some steps you can take to update your application to the latest version of Spring Boot:

* Choose a **supported Spring Boot version**: Check out Spring Boot Support Versions, determine the most suitable supported Spring Boot version.\n\n

* **Update Spring Boot version**: Update the Spring Boot version of your application. There are automated tools like Rewrite to help you with the migration.

* **Address code compatibility**: Review your application's codebase for any potential compatibility issues with the target Spring Boot version. Update deprecated APIs or features, address any language or library changes, and ensure that your code follows best practices and standards.

* **Test thoroughly**: Execute a comprehensive testing process to verify the compatibility and functionality of your application with the new Spring Boot version. Perform unit tests, integration tests, and system tests to validate that all components and dependencies work as expected."
spring-boot-to-azure-spring-boot-version.title: Spring Boot Version is End of OSS Support

links.title: Migrate Spring Boot applications to Azure Container Apps
Launch your first Java microservice application with managed Java components in Azure Container Apps

spring-framework-version-01000.description:
Your application is using the Spring Framework version End of OSS Support. 
Upgrading to a supported version ensures better performance, security, and compatibility with modern tools.
  1. Pick a Supported Version: Review the Spring Framework support policy and choose an actively supported version.
  2. Update Your Project: Change the Spring Framework version in your pom.xml or build.gradle.
  3. Fix Compatibility Issues: Update deprecated code, replace removed features, and ensure dependencies are compatible with the new Spring Framework version.
  4. Thoroughly Test: Run unit, integration, and end-to-end tests to make sure everything still works after the upgrade.
spring-framework-version-01000.title: Spring Framework Version End of OSS Support

unsecure-network-protocol-00000.description:
Using secured protocols such as HTTPS and SFTP (over HTTP and FTP) should now be the norm as applications are more and more exposed and interconnected. 
This CloudReady patterns looks for unescured URI in the source code or deprecated URI libraries. 
Ideally, URLs should be replaced in your source code by secured protocols HTTPS and SFTP (and ensure the infrastructure implements these protocols for the resources your application calls, uses or references).
unsecure-network-protocol-00000.title: Use of unsecured network protocols or URI libraries

links.title: SSH File Transfer Protocol (SFTP): Get SFTP client & server

utf-8-by-default-00000.description:
If not supplied, the `java.io.{{className}}` constructor uses UTF-8 by default. 
 If you haven't provided the character set, and UTF-8 is not appropriate for your class, then supply the appropriate character set to the constructor call.
utf-8-by-default-00000.title: The 'java.io' constructor defaults to UTF-8

utf-8-by-default-00020.description:
If not supplied, the `java.net.URLEncoder.encode` method uses UTF-8 by default. 
 If you haven't provided the character set, and UTF-8 is not appropriate for your class, then then supply the appropriate character set to the method call.
utf-8-by-default-00020.title: The java.net.URLEncoder.encode method uses UTF-8 by default
